All great points! I was just excited to throw up a repo as soon as I got code that compiled and was usable, but I had definitely intended to make it more robust and handle all of the above before submitting to [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi).

In addition to the above, I wanted to clean up a few other things bothering me:

1. [This `ti_find_indicator` call](https://github.com/cirla/tulipy/blob/0630f34208d3a3b9dd3914e2e86b9948c82013a0/src/tulipy.pyx#L22) is completely unnecessary seeing as we already have the indicator info [here](https://github.com/cirla/tulipy/blob/0630f34208d3a3b9dd3914e2e86b9948c82013a0/src/tulipy.pyx#L85), but [the __cinit__ function seems to insist that all of its arguments must be Python objects](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/cython-devel/2012-June/002734.html) and it complained whenever I tried something like:
  ```python
  def __cinit__(self, const ti.ti_indicator_info * info):
  ```
2. I have to take a closer look at the generated C, but I'm not sure if [this assignment](https://github.com/cirla/tulipy/blob/0630f34208d3a3b9dd3914e2e86b9948c82013a0/src/tulipy.pyx#L65) makes an unnecessary copy of each input, but Cython needed [this declaration](https://github.com/cirla/tulipy/blob/0630f34208d3a3b9dd3914e2e86b9948c82013a0/src/tulipy.pyx#L61) to let me [take the address](https://github.com/cirla/tulipy/blob/0630f34208d3a3b9dd3914e2e86b9948c82013a0/src/tulipy.pyx#L66) as the `inputs` param is a Python object.
3. I haven't done any kind of analysis to see whether or not any of this leaks memory.

avoid using that flag when using visual studio.

```python
def geocode(location):
    """
    Return a (latitude, longitude) pair for a location.

    >>> geocode("paris")
    (48.856614, 2.4699209)

    googlemaps_geocode = client.geocode(location) # client is a googlemaps.Client instance
    googlemaps_location = googlemaps_geocode[0]["geometry"]["location"]
    latitude = googlemaps_location["latitude"]
    longitude = googlemaps_location["longitude"]
    return (latitude, longitude)

- single read() for first seq number
- single read() next seq number
- final read() for packet & crc checksum

You propose to
- read() the sequence numbers, packet & checksum in single call

I didn't actually know about --prune when I made the original request.  I'm wondering if we can build on that term here, e.g. "prune local" or "prune merged".  --help can clarify that it would prune local merged branches and that it's safe to use all the time.  

I think having an option like git-up.prune-local is fine, but it's the kind of thing someone might be paranoid about and prefer to add to the command-line.  That's not me, though, so if you made it config-only I'd be happy.  Well, presuming all the deleted branches were logged so that I would have something to audit. 

In fact, it seems that the reflog for deleted branches is removed along with the branch.  I.e. it won't be possible to do 'git reflog <deleted branch>' after the branch is deleted, so it might be a good idea to do this to force a recent reflog entry for HEAD:

```
git reset --soft <branch-to-delete>
git reset --soft HEAD{1}
```
This guarantees that any deleted branch has a recent entry in the reflog for HEAD.  Except that is not a transactional sequence, so it would be better to:

```
$save=`git rev-parse HEAD`
# insert verification of success here...
git reset --soft <branch-to-delete>
git reset $save
```

The ignore option should make sure the specified modules can access the original time/datetime methods. Currently it only works for datetime.

I wanted the ignore option to work for modules that use the `time` module, so that these modules can call the original `time.time` method. This currently doesn't work, because it's not the `time` module that's patched, but rather the individual methods of this module. That means that, since the time module is shared among all modules using it, it's also being patched for modules specified in the ignore option of `freeze_time`.

This isn't gonna happen.

For starters, Discord does not provide audit log data. Which means that in order to do this there'll have to be a sleep portion and then an HTTP request. This is an incredibly expensive operation that flat out doesn't scale for people who have more than one guild in their bot.

Second, having to perform an HTTP request while dispatching is currently impossible given the current architecture of how dispatching works. So this request wouldn't even be possible.

Your best bet is to wait for audit log gateway events to happen. I've queried the developers about this over the span of a year and a half or so and recently they told me it's not going to happen in v7 of the gateway. So this probably isn't going to happen any time soon.

However, I think I may have come up with another solution to help improve things here.  I think part of the issue in these cases is that the data are simulated, so they are very structured on large scales: a square box with no additional masks.  This means the largest cells in the tree are all pretty close to the same size as each other.  

When traversing the tree, there is thus a tendency to make the same decisions about when to use these cells.  So if one pair happens to span a bin boundary, and thus steal some power from one bin into the neighboring bin, the same thing will happen for many pairs of these large cells, since the structure of the simulation means that the sizes and separations are all very similar.

This effect can happen on any kind of data, but it seems to be exacerbated when the simulation is a nice square box with a small w(theta), so the top level cells are all fairly similar in density.

Here is my proposed solution that seems to help, although it doesn't completely fix the problem.  I currently have 3 possible choices for how to divide a cell into its two smaller daughter cells, governed by the parameter `split_method`.  The choices are `mean` (the default), `median`, and `middle`, although I haven't found much difference in practice between them, so I always just leave it as the default value.

- mean splits based on the mean value of whatever dimension is currently being split (x,y,z,ra,dec)
- median splits at the median value of that dimension among the points in the cell.
- middle splits at the halfway point between the minimum and maximum values.

To help break up the structure of this kind of simulation, I'm proposing adding a fourth method which would split somewhat randomly.  

- random splits at a random point between the 25% and 75% points.  Similar to median, but not necessarily at the 50% point.

I've coded it up, and here are some results of the same simulation that Oliver sent me (plot above).
![mean_vs_random](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/623887/12438903/24c21096-bef9-11e5-89ba-2cb57f65744c.png)

I've plotted both mean and random split methods using bin_slop = 0.6 and 0.3.  The random splitting is clearly performing better than mean with bin_slop = 0.6, and it may even be better than the mean splitting with 0.3.  And random with 0.3 is better still, based on the smoothness of the curve. It would certainly nice to speed up greedy further. Just a overview of the current algorithm:

1. Initially assess all inner contractions (i.e. those with shared indices) of ``n`` tensors. If the contraction graph has regularity ``k`` (~ average number of indices per tensor) this has cost propto ``nk`` (ignoring the cost of filtering for inner contractions which is pretty negligible). 
2. Find the minimum cost contraction (time spent doing ``min`` is currently relatively negligible also) and add that contraction to the contraction list.
3. Modify the other possible contractions that are still valid with the new result, small  cost but propto ``n`` and have to this for each of ``n`` steps.
4. Compute the approximately ``k`` new possible contractions using the new result - also for each step. With this method the same contraction should never be recomputed.

So overall the main steps of the algorithm take ``nk`` time (don't think we could do better than this), but the steps involving modifying the possible contractions take more like ``n^2`` time. Here's some profiling of a very large contraction ([generated using this gist](https://gist.github.com/jcmgray/2fb88f1b8473eef5070316f02e26d66e)):

![screenshot from 2018-08-27 23-50-44](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/44690811-1c165300-aa54-11e8-801e-73bebb528370.png)

From that I think the biggest savings will had updating the internal format used to represent contractions. Including:

* storing the tensors in a dict (keyed by indices) rather than a list so that we don't have to manipulate index positions.
* not storing the full output set with every potential contraction (which then have to be modified). This is quite natural for small contractions, but when each of 1000 contractions is storing its 999 output index sets gets a bit inefficient.

 There might be other benefits to these kind of changes (like easier manipulation of the path once found, e.g. to ensure all constant operations are at the beginning) but it might also be quite an involved change.

This is a weird one.

Essentially, one can take any identity and make it the base for any other identity -- even has its own base. So pyangbind takes every identity and makes it a potential base. However, it also tries to be nice and figure out that if no identity actually used that base, then there's no need to have it there - so it cleaned up. 

In my example here ```base-identity-ref-stacked``` uses ```base-identity-ref-one``` as a base, which uses ```base-identity-with-refs``` as a base -- so pyangbind needs to keep ```base-identity-ref-one``` as a potential base, but ```base-identity-ref-stacked``` isn't a valid base.

```javascript
    identity base-identity-with-refs;

    identity base-identity-ref-one {
        base "base-identity-with-refs";
    }
    
    identity base-identity-ref-stacked {
        base "base-identity-ref-one";
    }
```

Now, why does this matter? Because the way that pyangbind cleans up the things that aren't a base is to check whether there's any other identity specifying it with a base -- which isn't the case for ```interface-type```. If you include iana-interface-type (which I think I did last time I was checking this module, because it's included from one of the other oc-interfaces modules) then ```ocif:interface-type``` is the best guess as a base for all of the IANA interface types that pyangbind can make, and hence it keeps interface-type from ```ocif``` around.

I checked that this works for both the individual openconfig-interfaces module, and the set:

```
[~/Code/openconfig/yang/interfaces(interfaces-42*)]
(9:37 - s004) piccolo> pyang --plugindir ~/Code/pyangbind -f pybind -o /tmp/discard openconfig-interfaces.yang openconfig-if-aggregate.yang openconfig-if-ethernet.yang openconfig-if-ip.yang -p ~/Code/yang/standard/ietf/RFC 
```

BTW -- having an identity that has no other identities that reference it as a base is a no-op statement -- it equivalent to specifying an enum with no values. If you try and set this leaf it will always throw an error:

```
>>> from tmp import openconfig_interfaces
>>> i = openconfig_interfaces()
>>> i.interface.add("eth0")
>>> i.interfaces.interface.state._set_type("foo")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'YANGBaseClass' object has no attribute 'state'
>>> i.interfaces.interface["eth0"].state._set_type("foo")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "tmp.py", line 1746, in _set_type
    raise ValueError("""type must be of a type compatible with RestrictedClassType(base_type=str, restriction_type="dict_key", restriction_arg={},)""")
ValueError: type must be of a type compatible with RestrictedClassType(base_type=str, restriction_type="dict_key", restriction_arg={},)
```

Note here that ```restriction_arg``` (which tells us what the keys that we can have are) is empty. 

This isn't a problem from a config perspective (since this is a state leaf anyway -- we need use  ```_set_type()``` rather than just ```.type = ...```) - but does mean that no backend can actually set an interface type here.

d74d83974f1efaf67c16cc2a3744616978425381 adds support for this kind of identity. It might not be a long-term thing -- perhaps pyangbind should throw an error saying that this is not a usable identity!

For a lens galaxy light profile, we exploit the uniform image plane grid and coordinates to perform over-sampling in an efficient manner. Over-sampling is the thing where we compute the pixel value at its coordinates, and then on a 2x2 grid, 4x4, 8x8 etc until the value doesn't change a lot.

Something that occurred to me is that if a foreground galaxy contributes to lensing, its deflection angles mean the lens galaxy's coordinates are no longer uniform, meaning the computation of its light profile using the routine above is numerically invalid. 

However, if the foreground lens is low-mass (which is expected in most circumstances), its effects will change the coordinates the lens's light is computed on by an insignificant fractional amount. Therefore, we should add a boolean tag to the Galaxy class called something like 'compute_light_in_image_plane', which if True uses the uniform image plane coordinates to compute the light profile irrespective of foreground objects.

The `--build-arg` values for ARG variables are accessible at build time to RUN commands. But unlike ENV variables, they don't become part of the image automatically. I assume that the password is only used at build time to download artifacts into the image. But there's no guarantee from Docker that the values of ARG variables are recorded only in the history and not in other places as well.

Regarding my comment on making context param optional. A safer approach might be to use introspection to see how many parameters are there. Since that would be a significant overhead it should be done only once during parser construction and all actions/recognizers should be marked (e.g. by attaching attribute `_pg_context = True`). Later it would be trivial to call actions/recognizers with the right parameters.

So I've just realized I've confused *column* checks and *table* checks. Django already supports column checks - retrieved from https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/fields/__init__.py#L667 - and seems to apply them even on backends where `supports_column_check_constraints` is `False` (like the MySQL one, for now). So we can go ahead and use them :)

MariaDB 10.2 also supports `json` as a data type, but only as an alias for `text`. So we'll add the `json_valid` check constraint for both MySQL (where it will be parsed but not applied) and MariaDB.

Encountered the same issue - This is a workaround. 

Had to take an extra measure to convert the **serverAuthCode** (from android application) to an access token which can then be used to get the user profile or access other APIs.

See: https://developers.google.com/identity/protocols/OAuth2InstalledApp
Using: https://github.com/PhilipGarnero/django-rest-framework-social-oauth2

I would imagine a similar approach for PSA (convert auth code to access token).

```python
class ConvertTokenViewCustom(ConvertTokenView):
    """
     Transform Google Token request to access token.
    """

    GOOGLE_BACKEND_KEY = "google-oauth2"
    GOOGLE_TOKEN_URL = "https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v3/token"
    # should match configured redirect URL on Google Developer console
    GOOGLE_REDIRECT_URL = "<REDIRECT-URL>"

    def get_google_token(self, server_token):
        params = dict(code=server_token,
                      client_id=settings.SOCIAL_AUTH_GOOGLE_OAUTH2_KEY,
                      client_secret=settings.SOCIAL_AUTH_GOOGLE_OAUTH2_SECRET,
                      redirect_uri=self.GOOGLE_REDIRECT_URL,
                      grant_type="authorization_code")
        response = requests.post(self.GOOGLE_TOKEN_URL, data=params)
        response_json = response.json()
        return response_json.get("access_token", server_token)

    def post(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
        request.POST = request.POST.copy()
        backend = request.POST.get("backend")
        if backend == self.GOOGLE_BACKEND_KEY:
            request.POST["token"] = self.get_google_token(request.POST.get("token"))
        return super(ConvertTokenViewCustom, self).post(request, *args, **kwargs)

```

I've dug deeper. It looks like this commit is to blame:

https://github.com/etianen/django-reversion/commit/71ad0226d5e17202e18d1a67df3f20d6db8ac68e

Reversion now includes a check that a model still exists in the database
before adding it to the revision. This is to fix cases of models being
updated and deleted in the same revision that can crop up in the admin
interface.

There isn't much point in saving a backup copy before instance deletion,
since every time the instance is saved it will be saved by reversion
anyway. All you're doing is saving a duplicate copy in the reversion
database.

Simply wait a random time between queries using time.sleep(). Note that every 100 queries, you should wait a little bit more.
This way has worked for me without getting 503 errors.

```
import time
from random import randint

count = 0

for query in queries:
    search_results = google.search(query,ncr=True)
    # do whatever you wanna do with the results, then

    if count % 100 != 0:
        nap = randint(45,60)
    else:
        nap = randint(180,240)

    count +=1
    time.sleep(nap)
```

Theoretically speaking: if data is not base64, just return the value with `to_internal_value` ...

```
if ';base64,' in base64_data:
    header, base64_data = base64_data.split(';base64,')
else:
    return base64_data
```

But it does not work with the current implementation of `to_representation`:

```
return super(Base64FieldMixin, self).to_representation(file)
```

The code above returns the full path including the host and media folder (with the DRF default settings). Therefore, the database will still be updated since base64_data does not match what´s been stored.

Example:

1. Database is empty. I add a new file (filename test.jpg).
2. Upload works fine. Database now contains `upload_folder/test.jpg`.
3. But the API response contains `http://myhost.com/media/upload_folder/test.jpg`.
4. If I send this value, the database gets updated again.

If I change the DRF settings to ...

```
REST_FRAMEWORK = {
    'UPLOADED_FILES_USE_URL': False,
    ...
}
```

... the API response contains the exact same value as the database and everyhing works fine.

Essentially, a more bullet-proof solution would be this:

```
if ';base64,' in base64_data:
    header, base64_data = base64_data.split(';base64,')
else:
    # we need to return what´s been stored with the DB, so if
    # UPLOADED_FILES_USE_URL is true, we have to change
    # base64_data to a relative path
    return base64_data
```

I pushed two things that make your script work. The first is using `min` to evaluate the filesize instead of `eval`.

The second is a quick hack to make working with unlinked files more convenient. As an optimization, we don't actually deepcopy the unlinked files since nobody can ever mutate them anymore, but it does mean that they're stuck with the state, and thus the constraints, from when they're unlinked. This is easily fixed by just reassociating them with the state you want to solve against, but this is unintuitive. Now, whenever you access `.unlinks`, it'll automatically associate the files with the current state. This will cause problems if you ever pull a reference to a file out of the unlinks list and store it elsewhere, and then try to access a different state's unlinks containing the same file, but that seems relatively uncommon.

boto3 requires both be present because [Glacier requires both be present](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazonglacier/latest/dev/checksum-calculations.html). Additionally, every sigv4 request requires a content-sha256, and Glacier is currently sigv4 only. We could remove that bit of the handler since now the request signer will add it in.

As far as providing it yourself, there's not really an elegant way. You would need to register your own handler that adds it in (just make sure to register it *before* `add_glacier_checksums`, or to remove the handler entirely).

Alright I updated the code such that ``set_result`` clobbers any exception. The reason that we do this now is that the ``set_result`` is only ever ran on the final task and if the final task succeeds. If that is the case, the entire transfer succeeded because the final task is only ran if its dependent tasks passed and it is always ran by itself at the end. As a result, a ``cancel()`` should not be reflected in ``future.result()`` if the cancel did not actually cancel the transfer and that final task succeeded.

FWIW in Debian we "rewheel" pip's dependencies from installed platform packages.  I don't know where that term came from, but it's basically a technique to turn the OS package back into a .whl.  We use [dirtbike](https://github.com/paulproteus/dirtbike) to do it in Debian, but the intent is for that tool to be cross platform (contributions welcome!).  In the Debian source package for pip, we indicate which Debian binary packages are used to create the wheels using a `Built-Using` field in `debian/control`.  That's primarily for dependency and other distro tracking.

So yes, there's a bit of a chicken and egg problem because we need a setuptools to interact with the platform build tools to build the Debian binary packages (.debs), but once we have those, we've so far been able to rewheel those dependencies.  Then we modify pip source to devendorize and import from those special wheels.  It does become a bit of a problem when pip adopts a new dependency, and theoretically dirtbike might have trouble rewheeling some future package, but so far this approach has worked for us.  Since Debian is in freeze right now (and so is Ubuntu), I haven't looked at any of the new stuff, but no doubt will have to adjust our packaging for the upstream-devendorized versions.

I do like the replies! And the idea of sending images is also pretty cool. 

What I'm less happy about is the fact that you are using the result of the experiment to send the image. I usually like to have the result be a simple number (or a few at most) that measures the performance of the run. This is very useful for sorting, filtering and will in the future help with automated hyperparameter searches. 

So I'd like to suggest that you put the image data into the `info` object instead. Maybe the `send_image` entry could be a dotted path into the info object instead of a bool? Such that e.g. specifying `send_image='test.accuracy_plot'` would access `self.run['info']['test']['accuracy_plot']` (using `sacred.utils.get_by_dotted_path`) and send that as an image. You would have to implement a simple `heartbeat_event` method that stores the updated info in `self.run['info']`, but it should be easy. What do you think?

Now that #1616 is clarified, it would be good to add a test to this PR that captures the subtle bug that the old query had when using a wildcard `parent_id`.

> select id, last_modified, data FROM records WHERE parent_id... collection_id=... ORDER BY last_modified DESC LIMIT 10 is a pure index-level read, it's always going to be fast. It doesn't even need to read the records table.

It happens to be that way sometimes, but in general this query may not be a pure index-level read, and it may need to read the records table.

> Anyway, there is a real way to prevent the len(SELECT) != COUNT across the two executions. You can use REPEATABLE READ

The only situation where you may need to manually resize images is when you deploy a Core ML and you make the predictions manually (as opposed to going through Core Vision, which can do the resizing for you).

We should include a snippet for how to do this as well, to make it easier to switch over to this method.

> I think things work fine if your install-from-scratch process is something like [...]

Unfortunately, that's not true.

When Spack installs things, they often end up in a fairly deep tree (spack includes various metadata in the path name).  Depending on where the tree is rooted, this can cause the `#!` header line of various things to become "too long".

Spack catches this and fixes it when it installs the package.

These fixes work fine for the `conda` that Spack installed, but when conda comes along and updates it the new copies haven't had their #! properly twiddled.

**Perhaps even more importantly**, the process above isn't repeatable.  Who knows what '...latest...' refers to, and who knows what '... update conda' installs?  The spack package tracks the digest/signature for the distro, every time you roll it will generate an error/headache (I'm looking at *you*, texlive...).  

**The versioned installers are really important.**

Jep seems like a good fit for your needs. Jep does support getting java objects back from python, [this table](http://ninia.github.io/jep/javadoc/3.8/jep/Jep.html#getValue-java.lang.String-java.lang.Class-) in the javadoc is the most concise summary of the default conversions. For more complex objects such as pandas frames you could use [PyObject](http://ninia.github.io/jep/javadoc/3.8/jep/python/PyObject.html) from Java, or you could write some glue code that would convert the python object into a java equivalent, since jep lets you import java classes into Python it is fairly easy to write a Python function that can convert objects between representations in each langauge. I don't know anything about pandas, but if it supports duck typing then it may be possible to pass java objects directly into pandas without conversion if they match the interface pandas is expecting. I would expect any of these options would be faster than doing a json conversion since there is no intermediary representation between python and java.

You can add `nrel_c` to `travis.yml` like this:

1. Add a script called `get_spa.py` to pvlib's top level. Here's an example, you can change the "name" and "company" fields to whatever you think is appropriate:

```python
#! /usr/env/python

"""
get spa c source from NREL for travis
"""

import requests
import os
import logging

# logger
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
LOGGER = logging.getLogger(__file__)
# constants
SPAC_URL = r'https://midcdmz.nrel.gov/apps/download.pl'  # spa.c source url
# register with NREL to download spa.c source
PAYLOAD = {
    'name': 'pvlib-python',  # <-- okay to set this
    'country': 'US',
    'company': 'pvlib,  # <-- okay to set this
    'software': 'SPA'
}
SPAH_URL = r'http://midcdmz.nrel.gov/spa/spa.h'  # spa.h source url
PVLIB_PATH = os.environ['PVLIB_PATH']  # path to PVLIB on travis
SPA_C_FILES = os.path.join(PVLIB_PATH, 'pvlib', 'spa_c_files')

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # get spa.c source
    LOGGER.debug('post payload to url: %s\n\tpayload:\n%s', SPAC_URL, PAYLOAD)
    r = requests.post(SPAC_URL, data=PAYLOAD)
    LOGGER.debug('post response: %r', r)
    # save spa.c source to PVLIB/SPA_C_FILES folder
    with open(os.path.join(SPA_C_FILES, 'spa.c'), 'wb') as f:
        f.write(r.content)
    LOGGER.debug('saved file: %r', f.name)
    # get spa.c source
    LOGGER.debug('get url: %s', SPAH_URL)
    r = requests.get(SPAH_URL)
    LOGGER.debug('get response: %r', r)
    # save spa.c source to PVLIB/SPA_C_FILES folder
    with open(os.path.join(SPA_C_FILES, 'spa.h'), 'wb') as f:
        f.write(r.content)
    LOGGER.debug('saved file: %r', f.name)
    LOGGER.debug('exiting %s', __file__)  # exit
```

2. Add a `before_script` section to your `travis.yml` to build the `nrel_c` binaries.

```yaml
before_script:
  - pip install requests cython
  - export PVLIB_PATH=/home/travis/build/pvlib/pvlib-python
  - echo $PVLIB_PATH
  - python /home/travis/build/pvlib/pvlib-python/get_spa.py
  - BUILD_DIR=$PWD
  - echo leaving $BUILD_DIR
  - cd $PVLIB_PATH/pvlib/spa_c_files/
  - echo entered $PWD
  - python setup.py build_ext --inplace
  - cd $BUILD_DIR
  - echo entered $PWD
```

@bolkedebruin Thanks for the discussing the problem with us. As I mentioned, this solution has difficulties in actual implementation. So I spent some time writing the logic out. I will have my working code pasted below and some pros and cons for this approach.
### Pros:
1. Re-use python logging module config parser (dictConfig)
2. Easy to extend existing Python log handlers by creating subclass 

### Cons:
1. [Critical] Need to change all operator code to use airflow.task logger instead of root logger
    1. This can be avoid by configing for root logger instead of getLogger(“airflow.task”), but how can webserver read the log as itself also uses root logger? 
2. [Design] Dynamically format configuration file defeats the purpose of using static configuration
3. [Design] `read` method in log handler breaks the handler abstraction
    1. Webserver doesn't know which handler to read the log from, if we have multiple handlers configured. 

### Code
task_handler.py
```python
import logging

class TaskHandler(logging.FileHandler):

    def __init__(self, dag_id, task_id, execution_date, try_number):
        # Do directory creation here, for simplicity let's just use the filename
        self.filename = "{}-{}-{}-{}.log".format(dag_id, task_id, execution_date, try_number)
        logging.FileHandler.__init__(self, self.filename)

    def close(self):
        with open(self.filename, 'r') as logfile:
            log = logfile.read()
        # Upload to S3/GCS
        super(logging.FileHandler, self).close()

    def read(self, dag_id, task_id, execution_date, try_number):
        # Parse the airflow.cfg and read from logging backend and worker machine.
        return "log"
```

As long as I understand SELECT ... FOR UPDATE and SELECT ... LOCK IN SHARE MODE correctly, they are locking just rows that are selected. 

E.g. suppose that first process runs the following code (trigger_dag.py) and DagRun.find() returns empty list:
```python
    dr = DagRun.find(dag_id=dag_id, run_id=run_id)
    if dr:
        raise AirflowException("Run id {} already exists for dag id {}".format(
            run_id,
            dag_id
        ))
```
It means that there are no dag runs for given run_id, so it will continue with adding new records. Even if SELECT ... FOR UPDATE lock is in place, the second process will  continue and hence it will also try to add new record to table => the second process will fail with exception.

That means that this approach will not protect the use case when two concurrent processes are trying to insert new records to the DB.

@Puciek it looks to be an error in the logic of our code. Under some conditions the code was running update_state even when celery is not running (which is a possible configuration of our application). Sorry for the noise

I think it's working as designed, but the documentation probably needs some clarification. Especially since `store_result` and `meta` are fields that are used in different ways in different contexts.

While a task is executing, the `TaskResult` is used really as current task state; holding information, temporarily, until the task completes.

So, when celery's  `update_state` is called from within a task if there is no `TaskResult` it is created and the `meta` information placed in the `results` field and with an updated status. Subsequent calls calls to `update_state` update the _same_ TaskResult, overwriting what was there previously. 

Upon completion of the task, the results of the task are stored in the _same_ TaskResult, overwriting the previous state of the task:  the return from the function in `results` and status set to 'SUCCESS' (or 'FAILURE').

The TaskResult's `meta` field is used for a different purpose (I believe to capture the results of child tasks?)

TL;DR

`celery/app/task.py` calls the `store_result` method on the _backend_ , not the manager. It is implemented by super class of `django_celery_results.backends.DatabaseBackend` and gets passed off to `_store_result` here: https://github.com/celery/django-celery-results/blob/master/django_celery_results/backends/database.py#L16. The backend, in turn, calls `store_result` on the manager, with the proper signature within the above method.

Hello Pim,

I had a look at your PR and added a few changes:

The first was with regards to your usage of ``std::function``, which comes with a rather stiff penalty (bloated code generation) on most current compilers. An older version of pybind11 (before the first official release) used it to bind all functions via ``std::function``. Switching to a custom solution in ``cpp_function`` that exactly reproduces the functionality of ``std::function`` reduced the size of pybind11 extension modules by a factor of ~2.5! Thus, my policy is to not use it anywhere within the core library (except in the optional header ``functional.h``). 

I think it's reasonable to impose that exception translators should be stateless, which makes things even simpler. 

Thus I switched from
```
std::forward_list<std::function<void(std::exception_ptr)>> registered_exception_translators;
```

to raw function pointers, e.g.

```
std::forward_list<void(*)(std::exception_ptr)> registered_exception_translators;
``` 

I also removed the ``exception_translator.h`` header and rolled it into pybind11.h (no need for a separate header if just one inline function is involved).

A patch with these changes and some documentation edits is here: [patch.txt](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/files/355568/patch.txt)

At that point, I unfortunately hit a snag. On Python 3.5, I get the following error message:

```
Can we catch a MyException?
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "example/example19.py", line 10, in <module>
    example.throws1()
SystemError: exception <class 'example.MyException'> not a BaseException subclass

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "example/example19.py", line 11, in <module>
    except example.MyException as e:
TypeError: catching classes that do not inherit from BaseException is not allowed
```

So it looks like there is no way around creating some kind of py::exception wrapper as disucussed here: https://github.com/lsst-dm/pybind11/pull/3/files.

However, a lean version of this modification would need to be found that doesn't create overheads for normal non-exception objects.

> I can't really think of any off the top of my head, but it would be good to come up with one for a test case to improve coverage.

Well, anything that had an stl container or an `std::string` member would be a problem.

I'll write up a test for it.

Possibly related to #1231: This looks like it might also be a symptom of argument reference policies - it is most like likely passing a copy of your vector to your function.

Can you try making your signature into `std::function<void (std::vector<double>*)>`?

I still would like to have a nice solution for this.
The problem is, that CMake does not really support this. The solution will always be like working against CMake.

Do you have similar needs? We have it working in our codebase, but I had to modify the pybind11 cmake scripts a bit (removing stuff we don't need). If you want, I can show you our current solution.

I don't like the idea of a runtime deprecation warning; it has the nasty feature of annoying the end-user rather than just the module developer (unless it's something pretty serious like "this is definitely going to break in the next release").  Deprecating it in the changelog and removing it from the documentation is fine with me, though.  The placement new stuff is currently still there, but I can't see anything that placement new would let you do anything you couldn't do with a (simpler) factory function constructor.

rec = content.storageResourceManager.RecommendDatastores()

This is tricky... the problem is that the last dimension (the one added by `'x'`) is broadcastable, but Lasagne only knows that the output shape of that `DimshuffleLayer` is of size `1` in the last dimension and prevents a merge with a differently-sized layer. An easy solution for your case would be making the output shape `None` for the broadcastable axis, but that would break other use cases (you couldn't add a `DenseLayer` on top any more, for example).
Your `ExpressionMergeLayer` seems like a suitable workaround. The docstring has some shortcomings, but I think it would be a good addition. Do you want to file a PR with tests and everything?

The alternative would be a `ForgetSizeLayer`:
```python
class ForgetSizeLayer(Layer):
    def __init__(self, axis=-1, **kwargs):
        super(ForgetSizeLayer, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        self.axis = axis
    def get_output_for(self, input):
        return input
    def get_output_shape_for(self, input_shape):
        shape = list(input_shape)
        shape[self.axis] = None
        return tuple(shape)
```
If you stack this on top of the `DimshuffleLayer`, it would work.

Maybe we should add an option to `DimshuffleLayer` to optionally set broadcastable axis sizes to `None` instead of `1`?

Hi @zhaojack, thanks for filing this. The behavior you're seeing is a consequence of pykafka's handling of the "funky" Offset API. The Offset API accepts timestamps, not offset values. To allow users to reset to specific offsets, pykafka attempts to treat them as timestamps and assumes they're offset values as a fallback.

Effectively, the offset value you give in `reset_offsets` is treated as the offset most recently consumed by the consumer. If you pass `latest_available_offset - 1`, the consumer understands this to mean that `latest_available_offset - 1` was the last offset consumed, and that `latest_available_offset - 1 + 1` should be the next message consumed. Since `latest_available_offset` doesn't exist on the topic yet (due to the way that the Offset API works), the consumer hangs until it becomes available. If you pass `latest_available_offset - 2` to `reset_offsets`, the consumer sets its "next message" to `latest_available_offset - 2 + 1`, which is the actual latest message appended to the topic.

The [documentation](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/A+Guide+To+The+Kafka+Protocol#AGuideToTheKafkaProtocol-OffsetAPI(AKAListOffset)) of the Offset API indicates that

> The response contains ... the "log end offset" i.e. the offset of the next message that would be appended to the given partition. We agree that this API is slightly funky.

For this reason, I realize that `latest_available_offset` is a misleading name. The function should probably return an offset one less what it currently returns.

See [the logic you're hitting](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/59c94b521056a02ae901d284d0da00aa75b68f9b/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L572-L592) in this example for more detail.

Yes, that's right. When stream id's are not specified, both in the DSL and
in emit() calls, it is assumed you are using a single stream, called
"default" internally.

But when stream ID's are specified in a bolt/spout's output, then
downstream bolts can subscribe to that stream using the DSL syntax Ron
demonstrated. To emit on that stream, the components pass the stream ID to
emit() as a kwarg. This mirrors the way Storm's Java API works, and makes
it so for simple topologies, you needn't worry about stream IDs, but for
complex ones, you can have any number and each with separate steam grouping
specs and distinct participation in the topology graph.

Brian, I appreciate that your thoughts evolve, but I get frustrated by what I see as a tendency to present your current thoughts as objective principles to be followed, and this issue seems like a prime example.

I've given two arguments for adding a label to the run button. I also have a third: I want to have at least one labelled button in the default UI to alert extension authors to the possibility. Discoverability and memorableness* are going to be more important for buttons added by extensions, especially when different extensions use the same icon from the limited set available.

(* 'memorableness' is something that always seems to be ignored in these discussions, perhaps because it's harder to measure than how easily unfamiliar users 'discover' a feature. This is also a more general concern about 'data driven' design: do we wind up optimising the things that are easy to measure, rather than measuring the things it's important to optimise?)

You've presented some data from a very small set that the label may not be *necessary*, which is different from demonstrating that it is actually *problematic* to add a label.

Following my comment yesterday, I read some blog posts from Microsoft about how the Ribbon was designed. One story that stuck out was that they considered demoting the 'paste' toolbar button, although they knew paste is used a lot, because they thought most users use it through keyboard shortcuts or the context menu. But when they looked at more data, even the small fraction of pastes done by clicking the toolbar button were enough to make it the most-clicked button. So they instead made it a big, obvious button with a label. Obviously we don't have the data to know if the same is true of our run button, but given complexities like this, I'm not inclined to place too much weight on what a small self-selected sample did.

The rest of your argument appears to boil down to doing what everyone else does. That's a reasonable starting point, but:

- From the list you present, what other interfaces do is not consistent. E.g. associating the run button with the individual cell may make it clearer than being in a toolbar at the top.
  - RStudio provides another example of a UI mixing labelled and unlabelled buttons. Thanks ;-)
- Other tools are probably also designed based on what everyone else does. Common UI patterns are of course helpful, but we shouldn't assume that what we're imitating has been carefully studied and thought out by someone else.
- Most importantly, the change we've made (adding a label) is minor, and does not seem likely to confuse anyone. If you look for a play-like icon, you will still find it in the toolbar, and then the text confirms your expectation. This is not some bold new UI paradigm that we're experimenting with, it's adding a short label to the same button in the same toolbar you know and love.

There's a separate question about which icon to use for 'run cell'. I think the rationale for the current choice was that it's more analogous to stepping forwards in a video than to playing it. Here, I think your survey of other tools is useful, and I'd agree that going back to the 'play' icon, which most of the tools you looked at use, probably makes sense.

How do you know you have the optimal hyperparameters for both _X_ and _Y_? Did you check them?

To conclude this problem, there is still debate whether this is a defect or not. It is suggested to use the POST method as it has higher timeout value. We can not up the timeout value to infinite because it will be prone to DoS attack and it's really hard to figure out how slow the customer's network will be. 20KB/s is pretty much very slow internet from server to server. In the near future we will proably host some api server in aws Asian regions to address this kind of fetching latency problem. But before that happens, it's suggested on your side to make your webserver as fast as possible for a fetch from US servers, either through CDN, or a proxy.

Okay so I found out a few more interesting things

* MSan is only useful if everything (including the standard C++ library) is built with MSan. This isn't a problem for ASan however AFAICT. So let's just ignore MSan for now.

* I think the assertion failure I was reporting is an artefact of Z3's build system. I spent an hour of so trying to reproduce the assertion failure and I couldn't. I then did a build (reusing a binary build directory I had used previously by accident) and then I hit the issue. I also got an error about using ASan when trying to run valgrind on ``c_example`` even though the binary was supposed to be built without ASan. This makes me think that reusing binary build directories doesn't work Z3's build system.

* The ``heap-use-after-free`` when building with ASan is reproducible (with clang or gcc) in a fresh binary build directory so this isn't effected by the above issue.

* I tried doing a 32-bit ASan (I tried both gcc and clang) build of Z3 and ran ``c_examples`` and no ``heap-use-after-free`` is reported. So it looks like the issue is only observable when doing a 64-bit build of Z3 with ASan.

Indeed, what @delcypher said! RNA only rounds away if it's exactly a tie. IEEE754 says: 'roundTiesToAway, the floating-point number _nearest_ ... equally near, the one with larger magnitude'.

IEEE754 defines dedicated integer conversion functions that don't depend on the rounding mode currently set; here that's convertToIntegerTiesToAway and convertToIntegerExactTiesToAway, both are defined as '... the _nearest_ integer ...' . Those functions do not exist in SMT, which instead has roundToIntegral that takes a rounding mode, but the semantics are the same.

MPFR's MPFR_RNDA is an extension and according to their documentation (Sec 4.4) it's 'experimental' :-)

The current approach to recursive function definitions is by compiling them to quantifiers.
Then these quantifiers are tagged in a particular way to ensure that MBQI (quantifier reasoning) uses them cautiously. In a nutshell, Z3 assumes that the recursive function definitions are consistent (do don't define f(x) = f(x+1)+1), and checks if candidate models satisfy the recursive equations at a limited set of values.

From python you can use the parser to get recursive functions:

       from z3 import *
       s = Solver()
       s.from_string("(define-fun-rec str-repeat ((a String) (b Int)) String \
              (if (<= b 0) \"\" (str.++ a (str-repeat a (- b 1)))))")
      for f in s.assertions():
           print(f.sexpr())

Hmm... Ok, so it sounds like the method I was using was the correct one. Unfortunately, it was definitely too slow (getting hit maybe 40000x in a 10 minute period). I went ahead and implemented my own close to O(1) lookup by creating a dictionary whose keys are the variable names, and the value is a list of the assertions. Keeping track of my own book keeping at constraint add and remove time has resulted in drastic performance improvements.

For reference, my cache dict looked something like:

```python
d = { 'var1': [ var1 + 2 == 5],
      'var2': [var2 + 12 % 4 == 4]
       }```

This allowed for a static time initial look-up of "is this in the solver at all", and then linear look-up not based on total equations in the solver to discover what equations actually used the var.

@graingert:

> out of interest what do you mean by "without binary wheel support", that's the only feature that pip-accel could provide on top of pip now that pip 6 supports caching?

That's your perspective, it's not mine. From the top of my head:

1. pip-accel implements a [KISS](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KISS_principle) *binary* package cache (that can also be stored in an S3 bucket) without the headaches of setting up a local devpi server and without the headaches of Linux ABI compatibility (think binary wheels hosted on PyPI containing `*.so` files).

2. pip-accel contains support for (automatic) installation of build time dependencies on Debian based Linux distributions (once those build time dependencies have been declared in a configuration file bundled with pip-accel; that file already contains quite a few known build time dependencies).

3. I use pip-accel as a stable API for pip in projects like [py2deb](https://github.com/paylogic/py2deb).

To summarize, if pip provides everything you need then by all means don't bother using pip-accel. If any of the above features are worthwhile however then you know where to find them :-).

It was probably broken before that change too... (your fix had a separate `except` clause that skipped the test). It seems that exceptions in non-blocking I/O would have triggered the faulty write error.

The timeout object returns `True` for `expired()` if the timeout is 0 (non-blocking I/O). This is fine for `read()` where it just breaks out of the read loop, but not good for `write()` where it would throw an exception (the return for non-blocking I/O is separate here). The fix is to skip the test for non-blocking I/O.

Hi @realbadbytes! The way you apply the attack is correct. Just the interpretation of the results is slightly wrong. The returned adversarial is actually classified as 781 (scoreboard) with a probability of 84%.

After running the attack using `adversarial = attack(image[:, :, ::-1], label)`, you can check it like this:

`np.argmax(fmodel.predictions(adversarial))`
**This outputs 781.**

Note that the returned adversarial has BGR color channel ordering (so for plotting you would need to reverse it, but not for passing it to the Keras model `kmodel` or its Foolbox-compatible wrapper `fmodel`). Also not that I am passing it to the Foolbox model that handles the preprocessing for us.

To get the the probability, you can apply the softmax to the logits returned by the Foolbox-compatible model wrapper like this: `foolbox.utils.softmax(fmodel.predictions(adversarial))[781]`
**This outputs 0.830603.**

Now you wanted to pass it directly to the original Keras model. To do that, you need to apply the preprocessing expected by Keras yourself, i.e. transform from RGB to BGR and subtracting the mean. You can either do it manually, or you use Keras `preprocess_input` function.

_Manually:_
The adversarial we have is already BGR, because the Keras Resnet model expected it like this. So all we need to do is add the 4th dimension and subtract the mean:
`preprocessed_adv = adversarial[np.newaxis] - preprocessing[0].reshape(1, 1, 1, 3)`
**And then kmodel.predict(preprocessed_adv)[0, 781] outputs 0.83060318.**

_Using `preprocess_input`_:
`from keras.applications.resnet50 import preprocess_input`
We need to transform the BGR adversarial back to RGB because Keras preprocess_input will transform from RGB to BGR. And we need to add the 4th dimension.
`adversarial_rgb = adversarial[np.newaxis, :, :, ::-1]`
**And then `kmodel.predict(preprocess_input(adversarial_rgb.copy()))[0, 781]` outputs `0.82709205`.**
Note, we copy the array, because `preprocess_input` works inplace, for whatever reason and so without copying, we would run into problems when calling it multiply times.

Finally, we can also look at the output of `decode_predictions`:
`from keras.applications.resnet50 import decode_predictions`
`preds = kmodel.predict(preprocess_input(adversarial_rgb.copy()))`
`print("Top 5 predictions (adversarial: ", decode_predictions(preds, top=5))`
**This outputs `Top 5 predictions (adversarial:  [[('n04149813', 'scoreboard', 0.82709193), ('n03196217', 'digital_clock', 0.03087672), ('n04152593', 'screen', 0.014944675), ('n04141975', 'scale', 0.01231501), ('n04074963', 'remote_control', 0.0091410046)]]`.**

_Summary:_
```python
import foolbox
from foolbox.models import KerasModel
from foolbox.attacks import LBFGSAttack
from foolbox.criteria import TargetClassProbability
import numpy as np
import keras
from keras.applications.resnet50 import ResNet50
from keras.applications.resnet50 import preprocess_input
from keras.applications.resnet50 import decode_predictions

keras.backend.set_learning_phase(0)
kmodel = ResNet50(weights='imagenet')
preprocessing = (np.array([104, 116, 123]), 1)
fmodel = KerasModel(kmodel, bounds=(0, 255), preprocessing=preprocessing)

image, label = foolbox.utils.imagenet_example()

# run the attack
attack = LBFGSAttack(model=fmodel, criterion=TargetClassProbability(781, p=.5))
adversarial = attack(image[:, :, ::-1], label)

# show results
print(np.argmax(fmodel.predictions(adversarial)))
print(foolbox.utils.softmax(fmodel.predictions(adversarial))[781])
adversarial_rgb = adversarial[np.newaxis, :, :, ::-1]
preds = kmodel.predict(preprocess_input(adversarial_rgb.copy()))
print("Top 5 predictions (adversarial: ", decode_predictions(preds, top=5))
```

Top 5 predictions (adversarial:  [[('n04149813', 'scoreboard', 0.83013469), ('n03196217', 'digital_clock', 0.030192226), ('n04152593', 'screen', 0.016133979), ('n04141975', 'scale', 0.011708578), ('n03782006', 'monitor', 0.0091574294)]]
```

def forward(client, update, to_ids):

    Forward an update to channel ids
    See:
        - https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/wiki/Forwarding-messages
        - https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/wiki/Retrieving-an-entity
    :param client:
    :param update:
    :param to_ids:
    :return:
    """
    msg_id = update['message']['id']

        #  bug:

        # ))
        client(ForwardMessagesRequest(
            from_peer=_find_entity(from_id, entities),
            id=[msg_id],
            to_peer=_find_entity(to_id, entities)
        ))

        LOGGER.debug(f'[x] msg_id: {msg_id} from {from_id} to {to_id} sent!')
```

For information, a Numpy array is not a bytes object under the hood, it has its own allocated memory. When calling `tobytes`, you make a copy of that data. This can be readily seen if you mutate the array afterwards:
```python
>>> a = np.arange(4, dtype='int16')
>>> b = a.tobytes()
>>> b
b'\x00\x00\x01\x00\x02\x00\x03\x00'
>>> a[1] = 42
>>> b
b'\x00\x00\x01\x00\x02\x00\x03\x00'
>>> a.tobytes()
b'\x00\x00*\x00\x02\x00\x03\x00'
```

PR #38 makes it possible to pass Numpy objects directly without calling `tobytes` first, which avoids a copy (and the additional memory consumption of it).

For exposure and posterity, here are some of the points I made in our offline conversation:

I support duplicated schemas and punting the problem.

I also support using a metaclass abstraction to define tables. My objection hitherto has mostly been in the interest of avoiding the dev costs associated with creating a mini ORM-like thing that's good enough to deserve to exist. If you feel that's something you want to do, then I will support you.

SQLAlchemy is something we considered. The problem with SQLAlchemy is, last time I checked:

1. We'd need to write the Spanner and Cloud Spanner support ourselves, upstream it, and wait for a release.
2. Whatever dependencies TensorBoard adds become dependencies of every piece of software using TensorFlow.
3. I subjectively feel like it's kind of a leaky abstraction. At least compared to Django's ORM.

My general stance on SQL is it's painful and there's few good options for making it not painful.

Another thing to note is the volume of data is going to be huge. Imagine a dashboard with 100 scalar charts, each with 1000 scalars, which means one million rows to show a page. Object wrapper library like SQLAlchemy aren't designed for that. I'm still crossing my fingers that the barebones db drivers (and the DBs themselves) will be able to marshal that many tiny rows with decent latency, and that the bottleneck will end up being disk (which is what this design has sought to optimize.)

Lastly, tensorbase will provide a high level Python API that abstracts the SQL. So one could argue that TensorBase itself is the ORM. Or I guess you could call it a TRM (Tensor Relational Manager) since SQL tables will map to tensors, not objects.

### IMO, this could work in 2 ways.

#### :one: "Icons" & "Brand Icons" files
Like others have mentioned just allowing for an "All icons" download or a "most icons" & "brand icons" will solve a lot of issues.  This lets it continue to work as it does today, and it allows it to be split up in a way to will hopefully satisfy the majority of people.

#### :two: More granular SCSS & LESS files
At the very least split up the icons SCSS/LESS files into `icons.scss` and `icons-brands.scss` to at least allow people to build their own copies.  Sure the actua icon data will still be in the font file, but this at least gets rid of some extra CS bloat that might not be desired by everyone.

---

Nice catch indeed, and I think the approach of keeping Agents in a dictionary is probably a good one, but there are some things to consider before adopting it:
* It will change the order in which Agents are called for step() in any models using the BaseScheduler's step() method. Currently they're called in the same order as they're added, but with this change they'll be called in an order that depends on the values of the Agents' unique_ids.
* More importantly, it will enforce the uniqueness of the unique_id each agent is created with. Currently, despite the name, nothing happens differently if unique_id is in fact not unique across the Agents of one's Model - indeed, the nature of each Agent's unique_id has no affect at all on the behavior of the model - so this change might break some existing models.

I think this raises an interesting point, though: why do Agents have unique_id at all? As far as I can tell, it's only currently used for logging, which purpose could be served as well by the id() function.

Question for @kmvanbrunt regarding aliases and multiline commands. Say I have a command called **alice** which is a multiline command. If I make an alias for **alice** called **bob**, is bob a multiline command too (even though it may not be listed in `cmd2.multilineCommands`)?

I think the answer should be yes. If so, the parsing logic should expand aliases before doing any processing to determine whether a command is multiline or not.

(I should say: specifically what I'm nervous of is that this might be a real bug in Python that we might hit again on random test runs depending on the phase of the moon etc. If so we need to figure it out and do whatever we have to to make sure it never happens again. Flaky tests are a constant issue for an I/O library like trio, so I'm super super vigilant about trying to keep them out. Anything else and you end up with like N different bugs that each causes a random test failure in 1/N times and it's unbearable.)

FWIW I really really like keyword-only arguments, and would be happy to see them basically anywhere in Trio.  They're harder to accidentally misuse, easier to compatibly change later, and clearer when reading calling code!  If it wasn't for the whole "invalid on Python 2" thing, I'd use them for practically everything - IMO two positional arguments is a sensible limit 😄 

I've also argued for more use of `partial(...)` before, so that doesn't bother me and I find the after version more elegant and explicit in the second case.

Looking at the source for sqlanydb, particularly the Connection methods __enter__ and __exit__, the use of with is supposed to be only for transactions, and not for closing connections or cursors. Not a problem at all -- we don't get enough user feedback right now, so we love tickets. (Seriously.)

What you're seeing is [by design](https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/blob/master/docs/architecture.md), but it could be a bad design, so I want to get more of your thoughts. What peru does when it fetches a module is hash all the fields into a cache key, and put the fetched tree in cache under that key. Then the next time you `sync`, as long as you haven't changed any module fields, peru pulls that tree out of cache instead of doing a fetch. Peru actually doesn't know anything about `url` or `rev`; those are defined by the plugins, and peru treats them as opaque strings.

There are a few upsides to this default: Plugins get caching for free. The `curl` plugin doesn't download a big file every time you sync just to see whether it's changed. (`curl` could have implemented that for itself, to be fair, but we wouldn't want different plugins to be inconsistent about how caching works.) It also means that most syncs can be very fast, because peru doesn't have to run plugins at all, much less hit the network. Finally, there's some benefit to stability, even if you haven't specified a `rev`: if `peru sync` is part of your build, you probably don't want new and potentially broken dependency code every time you build, and you want your builds to keep working if your network goes down.

The downside is that people who expect the behavior you mentioned aren't getting what they want. Right now, the way to get that is to wipe out the cache directory (`.peru/cache`) before you sync, which isn't convenient at all. At some point we should definitely provide a flag like `--skip-cache` or `--always-fetch` or something, which pretends that all cache checks are misses. We could also define a module field like `do not cache me: true` that works for all types, which would have the same effect in a persistent way. (Though maybe it would be annoying to type that on all your modules? If you forgot one, it would be hard to spot. Currently there's no notion of global configs that apply to all modules, but we could invent something...) I think there was another issue filed for this, but I can't find it. Anyway, do any of those ideas sound like they would work well for you?

@drastorguev: pickles from `dill` will likely be larger than those from `pickle`, given the module can save both -- unless there's some special case where there's a built-in `dump` function (like there is in `numpy`) that `dill` can leverage.   I don't think `gensim` has a buit-in `dump` function.

I agree with @matsjoyce, you seem to be hitting the size limit for pickle.  If you can't dump a reduced object (i.e. using `__reduce__`), then you might need to consider seeing if you can write to a `h5` file.  I have a pretty robust package to dump objects to HDF5 files, in `klepto`.  There's a less robust package (called `hickle`) that provides dumps to leaner `h5` files than `klepto` produces.  Those are just suggestions... I don't know if they will work for you.  Really big objects are hard to store with `pickle`, and if they can't be dissected, I tend to use another storage format (like `h5`).
That implementation is not exactly robust since double lines can occur in the pickled input. I think some integration into dill or pickle's format is required to implement this properly.

Hello @blackball ,
The officially supported patches are those of Ubuntu LTS releases 14 and 16 (and in foreseeable future Ubuntu 18). There are several community-contributed patches in `scripts` directory, but none of them I believe is suited for your needs.

Therefore I would recommend you to use `patch-realsense-ubuntu-xenial.sh` as a baseline as it already covers some Debian-specific steps , modify it and then port either 4.4 or 4,10 kernel patches to Debian 9.

The things to change/track in the modified script:
1. Linux sources should point to Debian repo rather than Ubuntu ([Link](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/development/scripts/patch-realsense-ubuntu-xenial.sh#L35));
2. Make sure to get kernel sources for the exact kernel version you're running (`uname -r`), otherwise the resulted drivers may not work.
3. Modify&Merge Librealsense Ubuntu patches into the downloaded sources.([Link](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/development/scripts/patch-realsense-ubuntu-xenial.sh#L68-L76)).
4. Make sure to install the prerequisites according to the [Linux Installation guide/Backend preparation](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/development/doc/installation.md#prerequisites).
5. Rerun the modified patch ones the above steps have been complete.

As far as next steps are concerned, it's probably time to start a new `hdr-header-props.feature` file and start on the header properties.

In general we'll want to stay to the "getter" side of things until that's pretty well fleshed out; the acceptance tests for the "setter" side generally use the getter side methods to validate they worked.

I'm thinking the getter aspect of `Header.is_linked_to_previous` is a good place to start. So something like:

```cucumber
Scenario Outline: Get Header.is_linked_to_previous
  Given a header <having-or-no> definition 
   Then header.is_linked_to_previous is <value>

  Examples: ...
    | having-or-no | value |
    | having a     | False |
    | having no    | True  |
```

That will get us down to the next step in the process, which is accessing the right header definition elements under w:sectPr.

After that, I'm thinking the next step is `Header.body`, which is the actual block item container. Something like:

```cucumber
Scenario: Get Header.body
  Given a header having a definition 
   Then header.body is a BlockItemContainer object
```

This will need to get more sophisticated later, adding a case for a header that *doesn't* have a definition and may need to create one, but this will be a start.

Want to work in that direction and we'll see where we go?

> An advantage of is over ==, aside from speed is that is doesn't unghost persistent objects. In some cases, unghosting them may not even be possible (POSKeyError) so is can be very valuable.

I think the point here is that `{un}registerHandler` checks if the handler is registered with `==` but then `unregister` uses `is` to find the actual handler - this disagreement causes `unregisterHandler` to return true (and trigger unghosting), while the actual handler is not unregistered. This is the bug that this pull request tries to fix.

We found that it makes it quite a bit easier to register (and unregister) handlers in unit tests that don't pull in anything besides `unittest.TestCase`. Also please keep in mind that `zope.interface` is used widely outside the zope community.

First of all, I'd like to thank everybody for participating in the discussion and for providing a lot of valuable feedback, and especially also for accommodating the very short timeline.

Open Source license discussions, especially permissive vs copyleft, have the potential to ignite very strong feelings, as the discussion often boils down to strong differences in ideological beliefs. Hence, I'd like to also say thank you for keeping an [open, considerate and respectful](http://invenio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/community/code-of-conduct.html) discussion. I believe it is highly important for a community and a healthy sign to be able to have these hard discussions with strong disagreements in an orderly fashion so that we can continue together afterwards.

I acknowledge that there are strong differences in opinions on this proposal, both outside but also inside CERN's Invenio community. I believe those strong differences boil down to above-mentioned differences in ideological beliefs. In both cases, i.e. adopting the proposal or staying with GPL, it will be a gamble where we can only speculate about the possible outcomes.

I've noted that the proposal will not have a direct negative impact on the existing community, in the sense that users already using Invenio can continue using Invenio, contributors already contributing to Invenio can continue to contribute. Long-term impact, as mentioned before, we can only speculate about, and there are pros and cons no matter which license we adopt.

I believe that due to the ideological differences it will not be possible to arrive at a consensus decision on the proposal, even if we let the discussion run for another month or two. Also, as already mentioned in the proposal, the final decision is taken by the existing copyright holders, as these are the legal owners of the code and the ones that legally have to agree to license change.

In that respect, CERN is the majority copyright holder of Invenio v3 having contributed more than 95+ % of Invenio v3 source code so far. The current situation with GPL plus shared copyright between potentially 100s of contributors is something that CERN believes is an unacceptable legal time-bomb because downstream it will be impossible to revert this decision. Even if the entire community later agreed to go with another license, a single contributor could veto against that decision. Having invested large efforts in Invenio, this is a risk CERN is not willing to take. Hence, we must take a conscious decision now, while we still have a choice.

We (CERN)[1] believe going with a permissive license like MIT is the right choice for Invenio for the reasons we have listed in the proposal and reasons given during the discussions. We believe an alternative, e.g. GPL with copyright transfer to CERN, would have a negative impact on contributing to Invenio as well as be very hard to administrate.

Therefore, considering that a consensus decision will not be possible, considering that a decision must be taken before it is too late, considering that CERN is the majority copyright holder and cannot accept the current license/copyright situation, and considering that the proposal will have no direct negative impact on using or contributing to Invenio for the existing community, I (as [Invenio Product Manager](http://invenio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/community/governance.html)) decide to continue with the proposal of changing the Invenio v3 license to MIT license. I take this decision only because I believe a consensus decision cannot be reached on the proposal, and thus I also fully understand that part of our community do not agree with the proposal. I am therefore also happy to take criticism, both public and private, and further discuss the decision, but I do believe I've tried to take a balanced decision.

The final decision still rests with the existing copyright holders, whom I will now take contact to get a legally binding confirmation that they agree to the license change.

Last but not least, I'd like to highlight what Tibor said: 

> building a healthy cooperating ecosystem may be more important than the concrete license choice

I fully agree with this statement, and I'm fully committed to building that ecosystem in collaboration with all of you.

---

[1] "We (CERN)" should be understood as CERN's official opinion on the matter as decided by CERN management. I fully acknowledge that some Invenio developers at CERN do not share this opinion.

However, I don't think we want the rng for the boiling to be associated with the object anyway.  Shouldn't that use the rng associated with the Atmosphere?  i.e. the same rng that was originally used to set up the random realization of the atmosphere in the first place?

Using an rng associated with an object means that you will get different boiling realizations depending on which object asks for that time step first.  Which won't necessarily be deterministic if we want this to work in a multiprocessing context.

Of course, I may very well be completely misunderstanding something, so please correct me if so.

@dhermes, @bjwatson, @lukesneeringer OK, I have the test passing, but to do so I have to straddle the fact that the GAPIC / gRPC bits sometimes raise a `_Rendezvous` w/ status code `ABORTED`, and sometimes a `GaxError` with such a `_Rendezvous` as their cause.  I think the APIs should raise one or the other, not a mix-and-match.

Specifics:

- `ABORTED` returned from the server during a `Read` API call is raised as a `_Rendezvous`.
` `ABORTED` returned from the server during a `Commit` API call is raised as a `GaxError`.

My hack to get the read-abort bit passing:

```python
@@ -312,7 +334,12 @@ def _delay_until_retry(exc, deadline):
     :type deadline: float
     :param deadline: maximum timestamp to continue retrying the transaction.
     """
-    if exc_to_code(exc.cause) != StatusCode.ABORTED:
+    if isinstance(exc, GrpcRendezvous):
+        cause = exc
+    else:
+        cause = exc.cause
+
+    if exc_to_code(cause) != StatusCode.ABORTED:
         raise
 
     now = time.time()
@@ -320,7 +347,7 @@ def _delay_until_retry(exc, deadline):
     if now >= deadline:
         raise
 
-    delay = _get_retry_delay(exc)
+    delay = _get_retry_delay(cause)
     if delay is not None:
 
         if now + delay > deadline:
@@ -330,7 +357,7 @@ def _delay_until_retry(exc, deadline):
 # pylint: enable=misplaced-bare-raise
 
 
-def _get_retry_delay(exc):
+def _get_retry_delay(cause):
     """Helper for :func:`_delay_until_retry`.
 
     :type exc: :class:`google.gax.errors.GaxError`
@@ -339,7 +366,7 @@ def _get_retry_delay(exc):
     :rtype: float
     :returns: seconds to wait before retrying the transaction.
     """
-    metadata = dict(exc.cause.trailing_metadata())
+    metadata = dict(cause.trailing_metadata())
     retry_info_pb = metadata.get('google.rpc.retryinfo-bin')
     if retry_info_pb is not None:
         retry_info = RetryInfo()
```
